claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 3 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 8 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 18 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)

expected_eligibility.name: Charlie
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about three children - Alex who's 3, Blake who's 8, and Charlie who's 10. They're all under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - thank you for letting me know about all three children! Since Alex (3), Blake (8), and Charlie (10) are all under 16, they fall into the same age category, which simplifies things.

Let me navigate to the next step in the assessment for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?

Given their ages (3, 8, and 10), I'd assume none of them are in paid employment - but please confirm this is the case for all three children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - none of them are working at all. They're too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes perfect sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) **not** receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

Again, given their young ages, this is very unlikely - but I just need to confirm that none of them are claiming these benefits themselves.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of them are getting any benefits in their own names. They're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently in an apprenticeship?

Given their ages (3, 8, and 10), I would assume not - apprenticeships typically start from age 16. But just to confirm: none of them are in any kind of apprenticeship programme, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're all in primary school, far too young for apprenticeships!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now I need to check your residency status.**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements? You only need to meet **one** of these:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and family (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? For most people, option 1 (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's straightforward! Living in the UK qualifies you for the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for the children:**

Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie? You qualify if **any** of the following applies:

1. **Living with the children** - They live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you for all three children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex and Charlie live at home with me. Blake doesn't live with me though - he lives with his mum, and I'm not currently contributing financially to his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - this is important information.

Based on what you've told me:
- **Alex and Charlie**: You're responsible for them as they live with you ✓
- **Blake**: Since Blake doesn't live with you and you're not contributing financially to his upkeep, unfortunately you wouldn't be considered "responsible" for him under the Child Benefit rules.

Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie. For Blake, his mum (who he lives with) would be the person who could claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?

For example, does another parent, guardian, or family member receive Child Benefit for either of these children? 

- **No one else is claiming** for Alex or Charlie
- **Someone else was claiming but has agreed to transfer** the claim to you
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

Which of these applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Alex or Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about where Alex and Charlie are living:**

Are Alex and Charlie currently in any of the following situations?

1. **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
2. **In care** - In local authority care
3. **Fostered** - In a fostering arrangement
4. **Living normally** - At home with you in a typical living arrangement

I assume they're living normally at home with you, but please confirm this applies to both Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Charlie are at home with me - just normal family life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Alex's or Charlie's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, you're not receiving any council payments specifically for housing or looking after these children. Please confirm this is the case for both Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - I'm not getting anything from the council for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's all the questions I need to ask. Let me now provide you with the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Results

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| **Alex** | 3 | ✅ **YES** |
| **Blake** | 8 | ❌ **NO** (under your claim) |
| **Charlie** | 10 | ✅ **YES** |

---

### Details

**Alex & Charlie** - You are **eligible** to claim Child Benefit for both of them because:
- They are under 16 years old
- They are not working, receiving benefits, or in apprenticeships
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- They live with you (you are responsible for them)
- No one else is claiming for them
- The council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance

**Blake** - You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake because he doesn't live with you and you're not contributing financially to his upkeep. However, **Blake's mum may be able to claim** Child Benefit for him since he lives with her.

---

### Next Steps for Alex & Charlie

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide documentation:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Birth certificates for Alex and Charlie
3. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit Charge
4. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 3) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally at home, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) does not qualify for Child Benefit under your claim because: Although Blake meets the age requirement (under 16) and other child-related criteria, you are not considered 'responsible' for Blake under the Child Benefit rules. Responsibility requires either (a) living with the child, or (b) contributing financially to their upkeep at least equal to the Child Benefit amount. Since Blake lives with his mum and you are not currently contributing financially to his upkeep, you cannot claim Child Benefit for him. However, Blake's mum (who he lives with) may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

Charlie (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, (8) Charlie is living normally at home, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children. Alex (3) and Charlie (10) both qualify as they live with you and meet all the eligibility criteria. Blake (8) does not qualify under your claim because he lives with his mum and you are not contributing financially to his upkeep - however, Blake's mum may be able to claim Child Benefit for him instead.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 3) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally at home, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) does not qualify for Child Benefit under your claim because: Although Blake meets the age requirement (under 16) and other child-related criteria, you are not considered 'responsible' for Blake under the Child Benefit rules. Responsibility requires either (a) living with the child, or (b) contributing financially to their upkeep at least equal to the Child Benefit amount. Since Blake lives with his mum and you are not currently contributing financially to his upkeep, you cannot claim Child Benefit for him. However, Blake's mum (who he lives with) may be eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

Charlie (age 10) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification, (2) Not in paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester), (6) You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie, (8) Charlie is living normally at home, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for two of your three children. Alex (3) and Charlie (10) both qualify as they live with you and meet all the eligibility criteria. Blake (8) does not qualify under your claim because he lives with his mum and you are not contributing financially to his upkeep - however, Blake's mum may be able to claim Child Benefit for him instead.
